title: House Prices and Rents
abstract: Variation in monthly metro area house prices unrelated to expected rents clouds the information about future rents in price-rent ratios and lagged changes in house prices. The variation in house prices unrelated to expected rents is, however, correlated across areas, and the problem is mitigated by measuring rent growth regression variables net of their monthly cross-section (across-area) means. This control for price variation unrelated to expected rents substantially enhances the information about future rents that we extract from price-rent ratios and lagged changes in house prices.

author.Eugene F Fama: Booth School of Business, University of Chicago,USA
author.Kenneth R French*: Tuck School of Business, Dartmouth College,USA

article.1 Data:
Issue Section: Article Editor: Itay Goldstein Itay Goldstein Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

A simple model says that in an efficient real estate market the price of a single-family home is the present value of the net of cost rents it is expected to deliver over its future life, P ( t ) = E t [ R ( t + 1 ) ] / ( 1 + r ) + E t [ R ( t + 2 ) ] / ( 1 + r ) 2 + E t [ R ( t + 3 ) ] / ( 1 + r ) 3 + ⋯ . (1)

In this equation,P(t) is the house price at the end of periodt, Et[R(t+k)]is the rational assessment of the expected value at the end oftof the net-of-cost rent the house delivers int+k, andris the discount rate that relates prices to expected rents.

Equation (1) says house prices are driven by expected rents. The goal of the paper is to extract the information about expected rents from house prices. The generic tool is regressions of rent growth rates on lagged price-rent ratios and lagged growth rates of house prices, dR ( t , t + T ) = a + bPR ( t ) + cdP ( t − K , t ) + e ( t , t + T ) . (2)

In regression(2),dR(t,t+T)is the growth rate of rents from the end of monthtto the end of montht+T,PR(t)is the price-rent ratio at the end of montht, anddP(t−K,t)is the growth rate of house prices fromt—Ktot. If the housing market is efficient, the priceP(t) inPR(t)reflects everything known attabout future rents and discount rates. In contrast,dP(t−K,t)⁠, the growth rate of price fromt—Ktot, responds to information about future rents and discount rates that becomes available betweent—Kandt. The prediction is that the coefficientsbandcin(2)are positive. Expected rent growth is high when house prices are high relative to rents and when recent price growth is high.

Models like(2)are typically estimated as time-series regressions for areas or aggregates (e.g.,Gallin 2008;Campbell et al. 2009;Cochrane 2011; many references in the review of Piazzesi and Schneider 2017). This approach runs afoul of a common problem in asset pricing research: variation in prices unrelated to expected cash flows that clouds cash flow forecasts in prices. (Shiller [1981]is the classic reference for common stocks.) Variation in house prices unrelated to expected rents can be traced to the usual culprits, namely, time variation in discount rates for expected rents or irrational market forecasts of rents. Either can contribute to the weak existing evidence that house prices contain rational assessments of expected rents.

The problem posed by variation in house prices unrelated to expected rents is recognized in the real estate literature, but no cure is provided. We offer a simple remedy. We have panels of data on the variables in regression(2), that is, time series of the variables for multiple metro areas. If the variation in house prices unrelated to expected rents is correlated across areas (e.g., price variation due to shared variation in discount rates), it can be removed in whole or in part via period-by-period cross-section (across-area) demeaning of regression variables. With our monthly data, this can be accomplished by including monthly fixed effects in a panel version of(2), or equivalently, by monthly cross-section demeaning of regression variables, that is, subtracting the monthtcross-section (across-area) mean of each variable from the monthtmetro area values of the variable.

Like others before us, we find that in the absence of monthly cross-section (CS) demeaning of regression variables, the evidence that house prices contain rational assessments of expected rents (and in this sense forecast rents) is weak. Going beyond existing work, we find that with monthly CS demeaning of regression variables, the evidence that house prices forecast rents both across areas and through time is strong. And it doesn’t much matter if we estimate(2)with panel regressions, area-by-area time-series regressions, or monthly cross-section regressions of theFama and MacBeth (FM, 1973)variety. Our evidence on the power of monthly CS demeaning to expose the information in house prices about expected rents distinguishes this paper from prior work.

TheShiller (1981)problem—strong variation in asset prices unrelated to expected cash flows—is a general phenomenon that has long plagued asset pricing research. Our goal in future work is to extend the approach adopted here to other markets in which the task is to extract estimates of expected cash flows from asset prices.
Our tests are for U.S. metro areas with monthly single-family house prices and rents. For rents we use the Rent of Primary Residence component of the U.S. Consumer Price Index (CPI). The house price data are S&P CoreLogic Case-Shiller indices, which, unlike most data on house prices, are based on sale prices. We study 10 individual metro areas and the aggregateAll, which we take to be the eleventh area. For rents,Allis the U.S. city average of the monthly Rent of Primary Residence component of the CPI. For house prices,Allis the Case-Shiller U.S National Home Price Index, which covers more metro areas than the 10 in our sample. Case-Shiller house price indices for most metro areas start in 1987, so the overall sample period is January 1987 to October 2022. Data for some areas start later. Our sample includes all areas with price and rent data for January 2000 to October 2022. We use seasonally adjusted Case-Shiller house prices. Rents are not seasonally adjusted.

The Case-Shiller house price indices are scaled so January 2000 values are 100. The Bureau of Labor Statistics scales each CPI rent index so the average for 1982–1984 is 100. This scaling of indices means comparisons of index levels across areas are not meaningful. We can compare changes in indices across areas, but we cannot draw inferences from across-area differences in index levels. Our tests examine real CC (continuously compounded) changes in house prices and rents, using the contemporaneous CC change in the CPI for All Urban Consumers (CPI-U) as the inflation measure.

The Case-Shiller indices are probably the best data on house prices. They are produced monthly from repeat sales of single-family homes, with controls for quality. (SeeS&P CoreLogic Case-Shiller Home Price Indices Methodology, April 2020.) A drawback is that the index for a month uses transactions reported in that month and the preceding two. This imparts a moving average property to the indices that results in spurious autocorrelation of monthly growth rates out to 2 months. It also smooths reported price paths, which means we measure the adjustment of house prices to variation in expected rents with error.

The rent indices also have measurement issues.Equation (1)assumes house prices are driven by expected net-of-cost rents. Our rent data are gross not net. For our purpose (studying the relations between growth rates of house prices and rents), this is not a serious problem if variation in growth rates of gross rents is a good proxy for variation in growth rates of net rents.

A house is a durable asset that produces services for many years, but housing services are perishable. Services not used when a house is empty are lost. Combined with costly search, the perishability of housing services produces incentives for long-term rent contracts. The result is measured rents that respond sluggishly to market conditions. This sluggishness is reinforced by the way the BLS samples rents. The index for each area uses six fixed sets of rental houses. A different set is measured every month, and each set is resampled every 6 months. On average, the 6-month gap delays reported rent changes by 3 months and clouds inferences about the adjustment of house prices to expected rents. In short, data limitations (for which we and others in the real estate literature have no cure) rule out strong inferences about real estate market efficiency. Despite these unavoidable data problems, we successfully extract information about expected future rents from house prices.
article.2 Background Statistics to Motivate the:
Figure 1shows real house price and rent indices and ratios of the two (PR) forAll. To simplify the plot, we scale all indices to 1.0 in January 2000. Vertical lines inFigure 1split 1987–2022 into the relatively quiet 1987–1997 period, the boom in house prices from January 1998 to the local peak in March 2006, the bust from the 2006 peak to the trough in February 2012, and the end-of-sample period, which is dominated by the surge in prices from the 2012 trough to the peak in May 2022. Given the attention paid to the 1998–2012 boom and bust (e.g.,Burnside, Eichenbaum, and Rebelo 2016;Nathanson and Zwick 2018;Kaplan, Mitman, and Violante 2020), it is interesting that the 2012–2022 surge pushes real house prices past the 2006 peak.

The difference between the volatility of prices and rents is striking. InFigure 1real prices forAllare far more volatile than real rents. The standard deviation of CC 3-month changes in prices forAll(panel A ofTable 1) is about 2.4 times the standard deviation of 3-month CC changes in rents. For 12-month changes, the volatility ofdPis more than 3.6 times that ofdR. (The regressions to come focus on 3- and 12-month changes in prices and rents.)

We do not show plots likeFigure 1for the 10 metro areas, butFigure 1is representative. The paths of prices and rents for other areas are like those forAll, and higher volatility of prices is a general result. The average of the 10 metro-area standard deviations of 3-month CC price changes in panel A ofTable 1, 2.35%, is 2.3 times the average standard deviation of 3-month CC changes in rents, 1.02%. For 12-month changes, average price volatility, 7.98%, is more than three times average rent volatility, 2.52%. Skipping the details, we can report that prices are more volatile than rents in every metro area.

The regression models are Panel One (simple panel regressions with constant intercept and slopes), Panel Mth (panel regressions estimated using monthly CS demeaned variables and no intercept), TS Area (area-by-area time-series regressions), TS Mth&Area (times-series regressions estimated using monthly CS demeaned variables), and FM Mth (monthly cross-section regressions). Slopes for the TS models are averages of area slopes. Slopes for the FM models are averages of monthly slopes. Total R 2 , the panel R 2 , is split between the contributions from CS demeaning ( Demean ) and the PR ( t ) and dP ( t − K , t ) slopes. The R 2 estimates are defined precisely in the appendix.

The higher volatility of house prices leads us to infer that lots of price variation is due to some combination of market biases in forecasts of rents and variation in the discount rates that relate house prices to expected rents. The result is variation in house prices that acts like noise to obscure rent forecasts in price-rent ratios and lagged price changes. This is a problem in TS (time-series) versions of the rent growth regression(2)and similar TS specifications common in earlier papers.

Our central hypothesis is that monthly variation in house prices and price-rent ratios unrelated to expected rents is correlated across areas and largely captured by monthly variation in the cross-section (across-area) means of the regression explanatory variables. If so, the problem can be mitigated by monthly cross-section (CS) demeaning of regression variables.

An extreme example illustrates the argument. Suppose discount rates for expected rents are the same for different areas, and any market biases in rent forecasts are also the same across areas. Then common variation in prices caused by changes in shared discount rates and market biases produces variation through time inPR(t)anddP(t−K,t)that is shared across areas. Monthly CS average values ofPR(t)anddP(t−K,t)capture the shared variation. When estimated on monthly CS demeaned variables, the regression slopes can focus better on differences across areas and through time inPR(t)anddP(t−K,t)due to rational estimates of differences across areas and through time in expected area rents.

CS demeaning is not effective unless large portions of the monthly variation of regression variables are shared across areas. Panel B ofTable 1reportsR2for regressions that include only monthly fixed effects. Monthly CS (across-area) means capture 46% (T= 3) and 48% (T= 12) of the panel variances of rent growth,dR(t,t+T)⁠; 64% of the panel variance ofPR(t)⁠; and 61% (K= 3) and 65% (K= 12) of the panel variances ofdP(t−K,t)⁠. In short, lots of the variation of the regression variables is shared across areas.

The key premise of our tests is that shared variation in the price variables PR ( t ) and dP ( t − K , t ) is due to variation in discount rates for expected rents and irrational forecasts of rents that do not forecast rent growth. We test this premise by regressing the time series of monthly CS means of dR ( t , t + T ) on the time series of monthly CS means of PR ( t ) and dP ( t − K , t ) ⁠ , Mn t [ dR ( t , t + T ) ] = a + bMn t [ PR ( t ) ] + cMn t [ dP ( t − K , t ) ] + e ( t , t + T ) . (3)

The monthly CS means in regression(3)are the values we subtract to construct CS demeaned variables for the regressions presented later. The estimates of(3)in panel C ofTable 1support the premise that the shared variation in the price variablesPR(t)anddP(t−K,t)is unrelated to rent growth.R2values (corrected for degrees of freedom) produced by the regressions are negative, and the slopes are within a standard error of zero. Thus, variation in the monthly CS means of each variable in the rent growth regressions captures much of the variable’s panel variance, but the CS means ofPR(t)anddP(t−K,t)show no power to explain the monthly CS means ofdR(t,t+T)⁠. From the perspective of the rent growth regressions, this suggests that variation in the monthly CS means ofPR(t)anddP(t−K,t)is noise that can obscure the area-specific information inPR(t)anddP(t−K,t)about expected rents. If so, we can reduce what is essentially an errors-in-variables problem by demeaning the monthly cross-sections of each regression variable. (This is the central insight of the paper.) We see shortly that monthly CS demeaning indeed strengthens the evidence that lagged price-rent ratios and growth rates of house prices have information about expected rents. (This is our central result.)

Table 1foreshadows two important contributions of CS demeaning to the eventual rent growth regressions. First, demeaning the explanatory variables,PR(t)anddP(t−K,t)⁠, strips them of lots of the extraneous variation (noise) that obscures their estimates of expected rents. We shall see that the result is large increases in the slopes for the two variables in the rent growth regressions. Second, and more subtle, lots of variation in the growth rate of rents,dR(t,t+T)⁠, is unpredictable at timetand thus missed byPR(t)anddP(t−K,t)⁠.Table 1tells us, however, that CS demeaning absorbs about half the variance ofdR(⁠t,t+T)⁠, which produces large increases in the precision of the slopes forPR(t)anddP(t−K,t)⁠. Slopes that are larger and more precise substantially enhance the statistical reliability of the forecasts of rent growth in house prices.
article.3 Setup of Regression Models for Rent Gr:
Exhibit 1 lists the variants of regression(2)we use to extract expected rents from house prices. Panel One, labeled regression(4)in Exhibit 1, is the simplest version of(2). Panel One slopes are the same for all months and areas, and One indicates that the regression has a single intercept. Regression(5), labeled Panel Mth, also has constant slopes, but it produces monthly CS demeaning of regression variables by replacing the single intercept,a, of Panel One with monthly intercepts (or fixed effects),at. CS demeaning eliminates the part of each regression variable’s monthly variation that is shared across areas. Panel C of Table 1 tells us the CS means ofPRanddPobserved atthave little or no information about the CS mean ofdRobserved att+T. After subtracting the noisy and uninformative CS means from the regression variables, the slopes from Panel Mth test whether the timetdeviations of areaPR(t)anddP(t−K,t)from their CS means forecast thet+Tdeviations ofdR(t,t+T)from its CS mean.

Exhibit 1. Regression models dR i ( t , t + T ) = a + bPR i ( t ) + cdP i ( t − K , t ) + e i ( t , t + T ) Panel One (4) dR i ( t , t + T ) = a t + bPR i ( t ) + cdP i ( t − K , t ) + e i ( t , t + T ) Panel Mth (5) dR i ( t , t + T ) = a i + b i PR i ( t ) + c i dP i ( t − K , t ) + e i ( t , t + T ) TS Area (6) Dmn t [ dR i ( t , t + T ) ] = a i + b i Dmn t [ PR i ( t ) ] + c i Dmn t [ dP i ( t − K , t ) ] + e i ( t , t + T ) TS Mth & Area (7) dR i ( t , t + T ) = a t + b t PR i ( t ) + c t dP i ( t − K , t ) + e i ( t , t + T ) FM Mth (8)

The Panel models in Exhibit 1 show the effects of monthly CS demeaning on regression slopes without the complication of slopes that vary across areas or through time. The two TS models in Exhibit 1 estimate time-series regressions for each area, with slopes that differ across areas. TS Area is a standard time-series regression. The Area label on TS Area reminds us that the intercept in a time-series regression produces TS demeaning of regression variables by area. Time-series regressions like TS Area are the common tool in the literature on the information in house prices about expected rents (e.g.,Gallin 2008;Campbell et al. 2009;Cochrane 2011), with weak results like those below.

TS Mth&Area estimates area-by-area time-series regressions on variables measured net of their monthly CS means. The purpose of TS Mth&Area is to examine whether, in line with our central hypothesis, monthly CS demeaning of regression variables improves on the explanation of rent growth produced by TS Area by absorbing lots of variation in house prices unrelated to expected rents.

The FM Mth model in Exhibit 1 produces regression slopes that vary through time by estimatingFama and MacBeth (FM, 1973)monthly cross-section regressions. The Mth label on FM Mth is a reminder that the monthly intercept,at, in an FM regression produces monthly CS demeaning of regression variables, which is our tool for exposing information about future rents in current house prices.

FM regressions appear in the real estate literature (e.g.,Titman, Wang, and Yang 2014;Case, Cotter, and Gabriel 2011), but not typically in rent growth regressions like(2). (Clark [1995]comes close.) Panel regressions are common in microeconomics, but they are uncommon in the real estate literature. (The price growth regressions ofCapozza and Seguin [1996]are an exception.)

The three Exhibit 1 models that share the Mth label use monthly CS demeaning to test our hypothesis that we learn more about expected rents from house prices if we abstract from price variation unrelated to expected rents and from shared unexpected changes in rents. The models differ on whether regression slopes vary through time (FM Mth), across areas (TS Mth&Area), or not at all (Panel Mth).
article.4 Rent Growth Regressions: Technical Det:
Table 2shows estimates of the rent growth regressions of Exhibit 1. There are four blocks of results, for forward horizonsT= 3 and 12 months for the continuously compounded (CC) growth rate of rents,dR(t,t+T)⁠, and lookback periodsK= 3 and 12 months for the lagged CC growth rate of house prices,dP(t−K,t)⁠. Each horizontal block has results for the five regression models of Exhibit 1. Skipping the details, we can report that results forT= 1 and 6 months andK= 1 and 6 months produce no additional insights.

The regression parameters inTable 2are slopes,t-statistics for the slopes (based on robust standard errors described later), and three versions ofR2(also described later). Again,dR(t,t+T)anddP(t−K,t)are CC changes in real rent and real price indices. In the regressions,PR(t)is the natural logarithm of the price-rent ratio, which is TS demeaned by area, so the mean of the log ratio is zero for each area.

TS demeaning of the price-rent ratio warrants explanation. Area rents are from the CPI, and area house prices are from Case-Shiller. Long-term differences between the sample of houses that produce an area’s house prices and the sample that produces its rents are likely to cause long-term differences among the price-rent ratios of areas. This is a data problem that can produce long-term differences in the level ofPR(t)for different areas that have nothing to do with differences in expected rent growth. We address the problem by measuringPR(t)for an area as the logarithm of its monthtprice-rent ratio less the full-sample TS mean of the area’s log ratio.

Panel One and Panel Mth estimate one regression for each combination of forward horizonTand lookback periodK. For comparison with Panel One and Panel Mth results, we need pooled measures of fit and regression slopes for FM Mth, which estimates monthly cross-section regressions, and for the two TS models, which estimate time-series regressions for each metro area. The regression slopes for FM Mth inTable 2are time-series averages of monthly slopes. The slopes for the two TS models are averages across areas of area slopes. In the discussion of results for FM Mth and the TS models, it is understood that slope means average slope. (Exceptions are obvious.) We interpret FM Mth, TS Area, and TS Mth&Area as panel regressions that allow the slopes to vary through time (FM Mth) or across areas (TS Area and TS Mth&Area) to increase the precision of their average values as competitors to the full-sample slopes from Panel Mth.

For Panel One and Panel Mth, we use the two-pass cluster method ofThompson (2011)to adjust the standard errors of regression slopes for contemporaneous cross-correlation of regression residuals and for the autocorrelation of residuals caused by overlap of monthly observations ofdR(t,t+T)⁠. As usual in applications of FM, variation in the monthly FM Mth slopes automatically includes the effect of cross-correlation. We use the method ofHansen and Hodrick (1980)to also correct standard errors of average FM slopes for autocorrelation of the monthly slopes due to overlap of monthly observations ofdR(t,t+T)⁠. By analogy with the standard FM argument, the standard deviations of the means of the TS Area and TS Mth&Area slopes automatically account for the autocorrelation of regression residuals. We add a correction for contemporaneous cross-correlation of residuals.
article.5 Rent Growth Regressions: Discussion of:
We argue that there is variation in house prices that acts like noise to obscure the forecasts of rents in price-rent ratios and lagged price changes. Our central hypothesis is that this noise in house prices is correlated across areas, and the problem it causes in rent growth regressions is mitigated by monthly cross-section (CS) demeaning of regression variables.Table 1provides background evidence for this story. We also hypothesize that shared unexpected variation in future rents reduces the precision of rent growth regressions. The regressions inTable 2are direct tests of the two hypotheses.

Monthly CS demeaning is a shared feature of Panel Mth, FM Mth, and TS Mth&Area.Table 2shows that the three models produce large increases inTotal R2, regression slopes, andt-statistics for the slopes, relative to Panel One and TS Area, which do not use CS demeaning to control for price variation unrelated to expected rents and unexpected variation in future rents. For example, for each combination of forward horizonTand lookback periodKinTable 2, the always positive slopes for the price-rent ratio,PR(t)⁠, in the Panel Mth, FM Mth, and TS Mth&Area regressions are more than three times the TS Area and Panel One slopes. The always positivedP(t−K,t)slopes are also much larger in the models that use monthly CS demeaned regression variables. Thet-statistics fordP(t−K,t)slopes from Panel Mth, FM Mth, and TS Mth&Area are all above 3.2 and often above 5.0, and thet-statistics for thePR(t)slopes are typically above 2.0. In contrast,t-statistics for TS Area and Panel One slopes are below 2.0, and most are below 1.0. Models like TS Area are common in the literature on house prices and rents, with weak results for rent growth regressions like those inTable 2.

In the regressions that use CS demeaned variables, extending the lookback periodKindP(t−K,t)from 3 to 12 months produces small increases (0.00 to 0.02) inTotal R2. But increasingKshifts explanatory power for rent growth away from the price-rent ratio,PR(t)⁠, to the lagged price change,dP(t−K,t)⁠. When the lookback period is 3 months, allPR(t)slopes are more than 2.5 standard errors from zero. WhenKincreases from 3 to 12 months, thePR(⁠t)slopes decline, and three of six are less than two standard errors from zero. The longer lookback periodKmakes the past evolution of house prices captured bydP(t−K,t)relatively more informative about expected rent growth, and the price-rent ratio,PR(t)⁠, loses some of its explanatory power.

The winning rent growth models inTable 2are Panel Mth, FM Mth, and TS Mth&Area. Among these three models, TS Mth&Area and FM Mth produce similarTotal R2values that are higher than those of Panel Mth, especially at the 12-month horizon fordR(t,t+T)⁠. There is thus some advantage in letting regression slopes vary across areas (TS Mth&Area) or through time (FM Mth), despite lost degrees of freedom.

Choosing among the three winning rent growth models risks making much ado about small differences. The important takeaway is that Panel Mth, FM Mth, and TS Mth&Area increase regression slopes,t-statistics for the slopes, andTotal R2by fitting the slopes around the monthly CS means ofdR(t,t+T)⁠,PR(t)⁠, anddP(t−K,t)⁠. This mitigates the measurement error problem that plagues Panel One and TS Area: strong variation in house prices unrelated to variation in expected rents. It is, however, interesting and perhaps surprising that allowing regression slopes to vary across areas (TS Mth&Area), through time (FM Mth), or not at all (Panel Mth) produces only small differences in model performance.

Using monthly CS demeaning of regression variables to extract the information in house prices about expected rents changes the interpretation of the regression slopes relative to Panel One. The slopes in Panel One estimate the relation betweendR(t,t+T)for areas measured net of its overall panel mean andPR(t)anddP(t−K,t)measured net of their overall panel means. In contrast, monthly CS demeaning of regression variables puts the focus of the Panel Mth, FM Mth, and TS Mth&Area regression slopes on explaining deviations of area rent growth rates from their monthly CS (across-area) means. This is perhaps obvious for Panel Mth and FM Mth, but with CS demeaning of regression variables, it is also true for TS Mth&Area.
article.6 InterpretingR2:
Table 2 shows three estimates of R 2 for the rent growth regressions. The most comprehensive is Total R 2 , which is the proportion of the panel variance of dR ( t , t + T ) captured by the PR ( t ) and dP ( t − k , t ) slopes and by fixed effects in regressions that have fixed effects, Total R 2 = 1 − s 2 ( e ) / s 2 ( dR ) . (9)

InEquation (9),s2(e)is the panel variance of the regression residuals (the variance of the residuals for all available months and areas), ands2(dR) is the panel variance of all availabledR(⁠t,t+T)⁠, theT-month growth rate of rents. Minor complications arise in aggregating the model fit results in(9)for FM Mth and the TS models because the panel is unbalanced: some areas do not have data for early years. The appendix explains how we adjust each residual’s contribution tos2(e)for overfitting bias caused by area TS demeaning, monthly CS demeaning, or both. The forecast horizonTand lookback periodKaffect the months included in a set of regressions, but for a given combination ofTandK,s2(dR) and the months that produces2(e)are the same for all models.

Total R2inTable 2is the proportion of the panel variance ofdR(t,t+T)captured by the regression, including variance captured by demeaning (fixed effects).Demean R2andSlope R2breakTotal R2into the part due to demeaning and the part captured by thePR(⁠t)anddP(t—K,t)slopes.Demean R2is the proportion ofs2(dR) absorbed by demeaning alone.Slope R2isTotal R2lessDemean R2.

The increases inTotal R2produced by Panel Mth, FM Mth, and TS Mth&Area relative to Panel One and TS Area owe a lot to CS demeaning. The contributions toTotal R2of the more extreme regression slopes produced by CS demeaning are modest. For example,Total R2rises from 0.07 in the Panel One regression ofdR(t,t+12)onPR(t)anddP(t−12,t)to 0.61 for Panel Mth, which adds CS demeaning (Table 2). ButDemean R2says that in this Panel Mth regression, CS demeaning alone absorbs half the panel variance ofdR(t,t+12⁠), leaving only 11% forSlope R2. The contributions of the regression slopes forPR(⁠t)anddP(t−12,t)toTotal R2in thedR(t,t+12)regressions are larger for TS Mth&Area (0.17) and FM Mth (0.19), but they are small relative to the 0.50 chunk ofTotal R2absorbed by CS demeaning alone.

We emphasize, however, that focusing onSlope R2misses much of CS demeaning’s role in isolating the information in house prices about expected rents.Demean R2tells us that CS demeaning alone absorbs about half the panel variance ofdR(t,t+T)⁠. The resulting reduction in the variance of thedR(t,t+T)regression residuals produces large increases in the precision of the regression slopes, witnessed by theirt-statistics. The larger and more precise regression slopes obtained with CS demeaning allow stronger inferences about the expected rent estimates embedded in house prices. In short, CS demeaning has two roles in the rent growth regressions: it produces slope estimates that are both more precise and more extreme.

Shiller (1981)argues that stock prices vary too much to be driven only by news about future cash flows. In that spirit, volatilities of house prices that are much higher than volatilities of rents in panel A ofTable 1imply that variation in house prices is driven by more than news about rents. On the other hand, the consistently positivePRanddPslopes and their larget-statistics in the CS demeaned regressions inTable 2say some of the variation in prices and price-to-rent ratios must be rational forecasts of changes in rents. TheSlope R2inTable 2estimate proportions of undemeaned left-hand-side (LHS) variances rationally explained by the right-hand-side (RHS) variables. In the undemeaned regressions,PRanddPexplain 0.01 to 0.05 of the variance of the 3-month undemeaned change in rents and 0.04 to 0.15 of the 12-month variance. TheSlope R2are higher in the demeaned regressions, 0.04 to 0.09 of the same 3-month undemeaned variances and 0.10 to 0.19 of the 12-month undemeaned changes.

What doSlope R2estimates imply about market efficiency? SinceSlope R2is measured relative to the variance of future rent changes, the answer depends on one’s view of how prescient an efficient housing market should be. It’s hard to fault the market, for example, for not anticipating the effect of the pandemic when setting house prices and rents before anyone knew about COVID-19. And since we don’t know what other information the market uses to set current house prices, such as a rational assessment of the appropriate term structure of discount rates, we don’t know whatSlope R2should be.

Finally, the slopes in Panel Mth, TS Mth&Area, and FM Mth focus on capturing the variance ofdR(t,t+T)after CS demeaning. It is interesting to consider whatSlope R2imply about the proportion of the post-demeaningdR(t,t+T)variance captured by the slopes. Demeaning lowers the variance ofdR(t,t+T)by about half (Table 1, panel B), so post-demeaningSlope R2are about twice those inTable 2, for example, 0.22, 0.34, and 0.38 in the Panel Mth, TS Mth&Area, and FM Mth regressions fordR(t,t+12⁠). And if one wants anR2summary metric for the regressions that combines the contributions of CS demeaning and the RHS variables,Total R2is better thanSlope R2, which misses much of the story.
article.7 Price Growth Regressions:
Variants of the rent growth regression (2) are common in the real estate literature. Also common are regressions that replace dR ( t , t + T ) on the left side of (2) with the CC growth rate of house prices, dP ( t , t + T ) = a + bPR ( t ) + cdP ( t − K , t ) + e ( t , t + T ) . (10)

Table 3summarizes estimates of variants of regression(10). The explanatory variables match those of the rent growth regressions. The difference is the LHS variable in(10), which is theT-month CC growth rate of real house prices,dP(t,t+T)⁠, rather than the growth rate of real rents.

The regression models are Panel One (panel regressions with constant intercept and slopes), Panel Mth (no intercept panel regressions for monthly CS demeaned variables), TS Area (area-by-area time-series regressions), TS Mth&Area (times-series regressions estimated on monthly CS demeaned variables), FM Mth (monthly cross-section regressions), and TS on CS means (time-series regressions of monthly CS means on monthly CS means). The data are monthly observations for areas. Table 2 discusses the estimates of R 2 .

The challenge in the rent growth regressions is to purge the variation in the RHS price variables that is correlated across areas and unrelated to expected rent growth. In statistical terms, the slopes in theTable 1regressions to explain the monthly CS means of rent growth are close to zero. The solution is monthly CS demeaning of regression variables. The slopes in the regressions to explain area deviations of rent growth from monthly CS means are much larger and statistically reliable (Table 2).

In the existing literature, price growth regressions like(10), with no CS demeaning, produce strong results (e.g.,Gallin 2008).Table 3documents the reason. In brief, the slopes in the price growth regressions for monthly CS means of the variables are similar to the slopes in the regressions for area deviations from CS means. Thus, in the price growth regressions, Panel One (no CS demeaning), Panel Mth (CS demeaning added), and TS on CS Means regressions (time-series regressions on CS means) all deliver similar slopes (Table 3). This is in contrast to the rent growth regressions in which the time series of monthly CS means of rent growth and the CS means of the RHS price variables are largely unrelated (Table 1). In the rent growth regressions, monthly variation in the RHS means is thus noise that obscures the relations between deviations of area rent growth rates from their monthly CS means and deviations of RHS area house price variables from their CS means.

Finally, regression(10)is a type of time-series model for house prices in which house price variables are used to explain house price variables. In contrast, the rent growth regression(2)targets what to us is a more interesting economic question: do house prices contain rational estimates of expected rents?
article.8 Conclusions:
The goal of the paper is to extract the information in the lagged house price variablesPR(t)anddP(t−K,t)aboutdR(t,t+T)⁠, rent growth fromttot+T. Previous work recognizes that this task stumbles on strong variation in house prices unrelated to future rents, but no cure is offered. Our cure is suggested by the observation that monthly variation in house prices and price-rent ratios unrelated to expected rents is correlated across areas and can be largely neutralized by fitting rent growth regressions about the monthly cross-section (across-area) means of regression variables. With the control for price variation unrelated to expected rents provided by monthly CS demeaning of regression variables, we find that house price variables have strong information about expected rent growth across areas and through time. The new approach, and the perspective it provides on the old evidence on the same issues, are the contributions of the paper.

Like previous work, we find that price-rent ratios and lagged changes in house prices forecast house price growth rates, with or without the CS demeaning of regression variables that plays a central role in the rent growth regressions. The explanation is that, in contrast to rent growth, regressions that use monthly cross-section means of price growth regression variables produce much the same slopes as regressions that use area deviations from cross-section means. The role of the price growth regressions is thus to provide a simple perspective on the special challenges of rent growth regressions.

Strong variation in asset prices unrelated to expected cash flows, what we call theShiller (1981)problem, is a general phenomenon that has long plagued asset pricing research. Our goal for future work a to adapt the approach used here to other markets in which the task is to extract estimates of expected cash flows from noisy asset prices.

Replication data and code: Replication data and code are available in the Harvard Dataverse at:https://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/RPRREH.
